---
title: "Lab 06: Entropy and KL Divergence"
---

::: {.lab-question}
**Fundamental question:** How much information do we lose when we use the wrong distribution?
:::

## Short Intuition

Entropy measures uncertainty. KL divergence measures the extra information needed when distribution `Q` is used to approximate the true distribution `P`. In VAEs, KL becomes the term that keeps the learned latent distribution close to a simple prior.

## Executable Code Block

```python
import torch
from torch.distributions import Normal, kl_divergence

mu = torch.tensor([0.8])
sigma = torch.tensor([1.4])

p = Normal(torch.tensor([0.0]), torch.tensor([1.0]))
q = Normal(mu, sigma)

# KL(q || p) for univariate Gaussians.
manual = 0.5 * (sigma.pow(2) + mu.pow(2) - 1.0 - torch.log(sigma.pow(2)))
native = kl_divergence(q, p)

print("manual KL(q || p):", manual.item())
print("torch KL(q || p):", native.item())
print("match:", torch.allclose(manual, native))
```

## Hands-on Tasks & Solutions

1. Compute KL for `mu = 0` and `sigma = 1`. What should the value be?
2. Increase only `mu`. Why does KL grow?

<details>
<summary>Solution</summary>

When `mu = 0` and `sigma = 1`, both distributions are identical, so KL is zero.

```python
mu = torch.tensor([0.0])
sigma = torch.tensor([1.0])
kl = 0.5 * (sigma.pow(2) + mu.pow(2) - 1.0 - torch.log(sigma.pow(2)))
print(kl.item())
```

Increasing `mu` shifts `q` away from the standard normal prior. The approximation now pays an information penalty for placing probability mass in different locations.

</details>
